name: typescript-strict
description: Enforce TypeScript strict mode and type safety. Use when setting up projects, reviewing code, or when type errors are ignored. Covers strict flags, no-any rules, and type inference best practices.
capabilities: Shows how to migrate from any to unknown types
Includes ESLint rules for TypeScript strict mode
Demonstrates type guards and null safety patterns
author: Doyajin174
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing TypeScript strict mode guidelines. No executable code, network calls, or file system access detected. All 56 static findings are false positives caused by the scanner misinterpreting documentation content as security-sensitive code patterns.
valueStatement: TypeScript strict mode catches bugs at compile time that would cause runtime errors. This skill helps you configure and enforce strict type checking for safer, more reliable code.
limitations: Does not automatically fix existing code
Requires manual implementation of type guards
Needs team buy-in for consistent usage

useCases.title: Configure New TypeScript Projects
useCases.description: Set up strict TypeScript configuration from the start to prevent technical debt and catch bugs early.

useCases.title: Enforce Type Safety Standards
useCases.description: Review pull requests for TypeScript strict compliance and educate team members on best practices.

useCases.title: Migrate to Strict Mode
useCases.description: Gradually introduce strict mode to existing projects with step-by-step migration strategies.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Strict Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me set up TypeScript strict mode in my project. Show me the essential tsconfig.json settings and explain what each strict flag does.

promptTemplates.title: Any to Unknown Migration
promptTemplates.prompt: I have TypeScript code with many 'any' types. Help me migrate them to 'unknown' with proper type guards. Show before and after examples.

promptTemplates.title: ESLint Configuration
promptTemplates.scenario: Set up ESLint for TypeScript strict
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure ESLint to enforce TypeScript strict mode rules. Include all recommended rules and explain why each is important.

promptTemplates.title: Type Guard Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to write type guard functions for complex data structures. Include examples with proper type narrowing and validation.

bestPractices: Always enable strict: true in tsconfig.json for new projects
Replace any types with unknown and implement type guards
Use type assertions sparingly and prefer type guards instead
antiPatterns: Using @ts-ignore to suppress type errors without fixing them
Casting to any to bypass type checking
Disabling strict flags to avoid fixing type issues

faq.question: Is TypeScript strict mode compatible with existing JavaScript code?
faq.answer: Yes, but you may need to add type definitions gradually. Start with strict: false and enable strict flags one by one.

faq.question: What are the performance implications of strict mode?
faq.answer: Strict mode has minimal runtime impact. Compile time may increase slightly, but the bug prevention benefits outweigh any small performance cost.

faq.question: Can I use strict mode with popular frameworks like React or Express?
faq.answer: Yes, all major frameworks support TypeScript strict mode. Many framework templates now include strict mode by default.

faq.question: How do I handle third-party libraries without type definitions?
faq.answer: Use declaration files (.d.ts) or the @types packages. For truly untyped libraries, create minimal type definitions rather than using any.

faq.question: What is the difference between strict and strictNullChecks?
faq.answer: Strict mode enables all strict flags including strictNullChecks. Individual flags allow gradual migration to full strict mode.

faq.question: Should I use unknown or generics for flexible functions?
faq.answer: Use unknown when the type is truly unknown. Use generics when you want to preserve type relationships across inputs and outputs.
